10.1016/j.jmp.2025.102955
No data was used for the research described in the article.

Knowledge structure theory (KST;  Doignon & Falmagne, 1985, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ) provides a valuable mathematical framework for the assessment of knowledge and learning. One of the central concepts in the theory is  knowledge state . It is inferred from responses to items and is defined as the set  
                         K 
                       of all items that an individual can solve in a finite knowledge domain  
                         Q 
                      . The knowledge state is a latent construct inferred from observable performance. Building on KST, competence-based extension of knowledge structure theory (CbKST;  Doignon, 1994; Düntsch & Gediga, 1995; Falmagne et al., 1990; Gediga & Düntsch, 2002; Heller et al., 2013; Korossy, 1997, 1999 ) takes into account the set of skills an individual has available, which is referred to  competence state . CbKST overcomes the limitation of focusing only on observable performance by explicitly incorporating the competence state, which is linked to the sets of skills required to solve the items. Within CbKST, these two constructs are characterized by two key mappings: the  skill function  and the  problem function . The skill function links items to skills by assigning to each item one or more subsets of required skills. There are two common types of skill functions, the so-called  conjunctive  (all skills associated with an item are necessary for solving it) and  disjunctive  (having available any of the skills associated with an item is sufficient for solving it) skill functions. The problem function, goes in the opposite direction, maps every set of skills to the set of items that can be solved using those skills, as defined by the skill function. Together, these concepts provide a comprehensive framework for understanding the relationship between an individual’s latent skills (the competence state) and the set of items they are capable of solving (the knowledge state), which is in turn inferred from their observable performance.

Abundant results have been achieved in CbKST ( Doignon, 1994; Heller & Stefanutti, 2024; Heller et al., 2015, 2013; Korossy, 1997, 1999; Stefanutti & de Chiusole, 2017; Stefanutti et al., 2023 ). One of its primary aims is to infer the latent competence state of an individual from the knowledge state derived from their responses to test items. The role of the problem function is critical. When the problem function is injective, then there is a unique competence state underlying the knowledge state. That is, there is a one-to-one correspondence between knowledge states and competence states.  Heller et al. (2017)  introduced a condition called the  identifiability condition , which is both necessary and sufficient for uniquely identifying the competence state corresponding to a given knowledge state. Additionally, other conditions, referred to as the  witness condition  and the  generalized witness condition  ( Heller et al., 2017, 2015, 2016 ), are sufficient but not necessary for ensuring the injectivity of the problem function under a conjunctive skill function.

However, as  Heller et al. (2015)  have demonstrated, the problem function need not always be injective. In such cases, the same knowledge state may correspond to multiple competence states. To address this challenge, the approach is to work with the equivalence classes induced by the problem function. Each such class encompasses all competence states that delineate the same knowledge state.

On the one hand, under the assumption of conjunctiveness, the equivalence class is closed under intersection and thus contains its greatest lower bound, termed the  floor  by  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017) . The floor represents the collection of all minimally sufficient skills required to delineate the knowledge state induced by the problem function when applied to any member of the corresponding equivalence class. The concept of the floor becomes particularly significant when knowledge states and competence states do not exhibit a one-to-one correspondence. For every knowledge state, there exists exactly one floor that delineates it. In other words, a knowledge state provides unambiguous information about the minimal subset of skills an individual has mastered, as represented by the corresponding floor.

On the other hand, the lack of a one-to-one correspondence makes the equivalence classes of competence states induced by the problem function worthy of study in their own right. For a collection of competence states, the greater the number of equivalence classes derived from the problem function, the lower the informational uncertainty regarding the competence state underlying the knowledge state, this equates to greater informativeness about an individual’s competence state. Competence-based test development (CbTD;  Anselmi et al., 2022, 2024 ) is a recent and innovative approach to designing tests that are as informative as possible about individuals’ competence state. Rooted in the theoretical framework of CbKST,  Anselmi et al. (2022)  outlined procedures for test development in three key contexts: the  construction of a test from scratch , the  improvement of an existing test , and the  shortening of an existing test . Each of these procedures contributes to maximize informativeness about individuals’ competence states. The comparison of informativeness between different models forms the foundation of this article. We establish a connection between this comparison and the concept of floors introduced earlier. Under specific restrictions on the collection of competence states, the informativeness of two conjunctive models can be represented through the set inclusion of their corresponding floors. Additionally, due to the one-to-one relationship between a knowledge state and the floor that delineates it, we reformulate this relationship through the application of ordered sets. This allows difference in informativeness to be expressed through order-preserving maps with distinct characteristics, offering a novel approach to comparing different models. Building on informativeness, reducibility ( Anselmi et al., 2022 ) pertains to the possibility of removing items without making the model less informative. A reducible model allows for a proper subset of the domain while preserving its informativeness. In contrast, irreducibility refers to the inability to remove items without diminishing the model’s informativeness. Irreducibility is particularly significant in practical applications, such as educational assessment, where the goal is to efficiently measure students’ competencies by selecting and administering the minimal number of questions necessary, while avoiding redundant ones. In this paper, we address these challenges and provide solutions to optimize test design.

This paper is organized as follows. Section  2  introduces the foundational concepts. Section  3  explores the informativeness of conjunctive competence models through two representation methods: one based on the set inclusion of floors, and the other utilizing mappings between two specially ordered sets. The disjunctive competence models and general competence models are also examined. Section  4  focuses on the reducibility of conjunctive competence models, providing characterizations for both reducibility and irreducibility. Finally, Section  5  concludes the paper.

It should be noted that, when moving from the full power set

                         C 
                       is floor-inclusive if and only if every equivalence class contains a unique minimal element.

The following definition, as proposed by  Anselmi et al. (2022) , provides a comparison of informativeness between two competence models.

As per the above definition, consider two competence models  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      , which share the same competence structure but may have different items. If there are distinct competence states that delineate the same knowledge state via  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       and not via  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      , and never the reverse, it means  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       can uncover a competence state from a given knowledge state more effectively. In this case,  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       is said to be more informative than  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      . If  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       is at least as informative as  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       is said to be weakly more informative than  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      . Finally, if two distinct competence states delineate the same knowledge state by one model, then the other model also holds, then  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       are said to be equally informative.

This subsection studies two specific types of competence models: conjunctive and disjunctive competence models, we mainly discuss the conjunctive situation and present the disjunctive situation as the corollary.

In the following proposition, we will see that  Definition 1  is closely related to the concept of floors.

                                  q

                          denote the collection of subsets of skills that are minimally sufficient for solving some item. Lemma 1 in  Heller et al. (2017)  shows that for any competence model

                                     p 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . In the case of a conjunctive skill function it means for every

                               ∈

                                           q

                                  p

Proposition 2

Proposition 2 characterizes when  
                            C 
                          satisfies  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               C 
                            
                          and is closed under union, in which case  
                            
                               T 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ⋃ 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                     ∈ 
                                     p 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        T 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                            
                          holds for all  
                            
                               T 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , and consequently  
                            C 
                          is floor-inclusive. However, it should be noted that the converse result may not hold, as demonstrated in the example below.

Example 2 
                            Consider the competence model  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     Q 
                                     , 
                                     S 
                                     , 
                                     C 
                                     , 
                                     μ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                with  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        2 
                                        , 
                                        3 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               ,  
                                  
                                     S 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     C 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        0̸ 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                        b 
                                        c 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               , the mappings  
                                  μ 
                                is defined by  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           μ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              1 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           
                                              { 
                                              a 
                                              } 
                                           
                                           , 
                                           μ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              2 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           
                                              { 
                                              b 
                                              } 
                                           
                                           , 
                                           μ 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              3 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           
                                              { 
                                              c 
                                              } 
                                           
                                           , 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               then

When examining the structure of the general competence set  
                            C 
                         , which is distinct from  
                            
                               
                                  2

                                     μ 
                                  
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               C 
                            
                          and that  
                            C 
                          possesses the union-closure property. Throughout our analysis, we consistently uphold these two conditions for  
                            C 
                          due to the following rationale. Firstly, as established by  Proposition 1 ,  
                            C 
                          must be floor-inclusive. Additionally, Lemma 1 from  Heller et al. (2017)  demonstrates that for any  
                            
                               T 
                               ∈

                                     μ

                          is minimal within its equivalence class

                                  p 
                               
                            
                         , this substantiates the rationale for our assumption that

                                     μ 
                                  
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               C 
                            
                         . Secondly, the ability to express any floor as a union of competencies under  
                            μ 
                          is pivotal for deriving our primary results (specifically,

                               =

                                     q

                            C 
                          ensures the validity of this expression. Without this property, as illustrated in the subsequent example, such an expression may not be valid.

Theorem 1 
                            
                               Let

While  Theorem 1  provides a formal criterion for informativeness based on the set inclusion of floors, this approach can be abstract. The ordered set  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     Γ 
                                  
                                  
                                     c 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          offers a more structural and intuitive perspective. It translates the set-theoretic relationship into a concrete partially ordered set, whose structure can be directly visualized via Hasse diagrams (see  Example 5 ). These diagrams make the hierarchical and comparative nature of informativeness immediately apparent. Furthermore, this formulation connects informativeness to the established framework of order theory, facilitating deeper structural analysis.

A binary relation  
                            ⩽ 
                          on a set  
                            M 
                          is called an  order  (or  partial order ) if for all  
                            
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               , 
                               z 
                               ∈ 
                               M 
                            
                         :  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                               x 
                               ⩽ 
                               x 
                            
                         ;  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                               x 
                               ⩽ 
                               y 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               y 
                               ⩽ 
                               x 
                            
                          imply  
                            
                               x 
                               = 
                               y 
                            
                         ;  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                               x 
                               ⩽ 
                               y 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               y 
                               ⩽ 
                               z 
                            
                          imply  
                            
                               x 
                               ⩽ 
                               z 
                            
                         . These conditions are referred to, respectively, as reflexivity, antisymmetry and transitivity. A set  
                            M 
                          equipped with an order relation  
                            ⩽ 
                          is said to be an  ordered set  (or  partially ordered set )  
                            
                               ( 
                               M 
                               , 
                               ⩽ 
                               ) 
                            
                         . In the case of  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     Γ 
                                  
                                  
                                     c 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          mentioned above, denote  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⩽ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          if and only if  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            
                          for all  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , it is easily verified that  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     Γ 
                                  
                                  
                                     c 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                               ⩽ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is an ordered set since set inclusion is an order and the problem function  
                            p 
                          is monotonic.

Maps between two ordered sets illustrate structure-preserving properties through the concepts of order-embedding and order-isomorphism. To further represent the informativeness, and based on the original definition by  Davey and Priestley (2002) , we introduce an additional concept called “proper order-embedding” to the following definition.

Definition 2 
                            Let  
                                  M 
                                and  
                                  N 
                                be two ordered sets. A map  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     : 
                                     M 
                                     → 
                                     N 
                                  
                                is said to be 
                            (i) an  order-embedding  if  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     ⩽ 
                                     y 
                                  
                                in  
                                  M 
                                if and only if  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ⩽ 
                                     φ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                in  
                                  N 
                               ; 
                            (ii) an  order-isomorphism  if it is an order-embedding which maps  
                                  M 
                                onto  
                                  N 
                               ; 
                            (iii) a  proper order-embedding  if it is an order-embedding and  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        M 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ⊂ 
                                     N 
                                  
                               .

Let  
                                  M 
                                and  
                                  N 
                                be two ordered sets. A map  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     : 
                                     M 
                                     → 
                                     N 
                                  
                                is said to be

(i) an  order-embedding  if  
                                  
                                     x 
                                     ⩽ 
                                     y 
                                  
                                in  
                                  M 
                                if and only if  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ⩽ 
                                     φ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                in  
                                  N 
                               ;

(ii) an  order-isomorphism  if it is an order-embedding which maps  
                                  M 
                                onto  
                                  N 
                               ;

(iii) a  proper order-embedding  if it is an order-embedding and  
                                  
                                     φ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        M 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ⊂ 
                                     N 
                                  
                               .

Remark 1 
                            
                               
                               If  
                                  
                                     C 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                        
                                           S 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                for any skill set  
                                  S 
                               , then  
                                  C 
                                automatically satisfies ESCC, so all the aforementioned results remain valid when considering all the subsets of  
                                  S 
                                to form competence structure.

If  
                                  
                                     C 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                        
                                           S 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                for any skill set  
                                  S 
                               , then  
                                  C 
                                automatically satisfies ESCC, so all the aforementioned results remain valid when considering all the subsets of  
                                  S 
                                to form competence structure.

Before we conclude this subsection, it should be noted that all the above results can be extended to disjunctive competence models. In fact, a well-known duality exists between these two types of competence models. Based on the following relationship, the corresponding results for the disjunctive case can be obtained through transformation into the conjunctive case.

Theorem 3

Proof 
                            Similar to the proof of  Theorem 2 , we can prove this by applying  Proposition 5 . □

Similar to the proof of  Theorem 2 , we can prove this by applying  Proposition 5 . □

Within the power set  
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                         , the conjunctive (respectively, disjunctive) problem function satisfies closure under intersection (resp. union). Each equivalence class of the competence states via the conjunctive (resp. disjunctive) model has a unique floor (resp.ceiling), corresponding to the greatest lower bound (resp. least upper bound) of the class. However, for arbitrary competence models in the general case, the problem function lacks both intersection and union closure, this structural limitation implies that the equivalence classes of competence states generally lack unique floors or ceilings. To overcome this deficiency,  Wang et al. (2025)  introduced the concepts of the bottom space and the top space for general competence models. Through a slight modification to the competence structure  
                            C 
                          (where  
                            
                               C 
                               ≠ 
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                               
                            
                         ), we propose the following refined definitions based on Wang et al.’s original concepts.

Definition 3 
                            Let  
                                  
                                     M 
                                     =

                                        S 
                                        , 
                                        C 
                                        , 
                                        μ

                                be a competence model with the problem function  
                                  p 
                               , and for any  
                                  
                                     C 
                                     ∈ 
                                     C

                                           [ 
                                           C 
                                           ] 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                                is the equivalence class of competence state  
                                  C 
                               . 
                            (i) The set

Theorem 1  (resp.  Theorem 3 ) shows when  
                            C 
                          satisfies ESCC (resp. CSCC), the informativeness of the conjunctive (resp. disjunctive) competence models can be represented by the floor set  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  C 
                               
                            
                          (resp. ceiling set  
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  C 
                               
                            
                         ). Nevertheless, in general competence models, this result does not hold, even when we restrict our consideration to the power set  
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                         .

Example 7 
                            Consider two competence models

Remark 2 
                            It is reasonable to conclude that  Theorem 1  or  Theorem 3  no longer hold in the general competence models. This is because the bottom space (resp. top space) is derived by identifying the minimal (resp. maximal) elements within the equivalence class, whereas in the conjunctive (resp. disjunctive) case, the floor (resp. ceiling) is constructed through intersection (resp. union) operations.

It is reasonable to conclude that  Theorem 1  or  Theorem 3  no longer hold in the general competence models. This is because the bottom space (resp. top space) is derived by identifying the minimal (resp. maximal) elements within the equivalence class, whereas in the conjunctive (resp. disjunctive) case, the floor (resp. ceiling) is constructed through intersection (resp. union) operations.

A competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            C 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is said to be  irreducible  if it is more informative than any proper domain restriction of it ( Anselmi et al., 2022 ). In other words, irreducibility implies that each item in  
                         Q 
                       is necessary to distinguish any two competence states from the knowledge states. Conversely, a  reducible  competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            C 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       allows for a proper domain restriction  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            C 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       such that the two models are equally informative. Drawing upon this, in the case of conjunctiveness, one is interested in characterizing relevant properties of reducible competence models in terms of informativeness, which connects to floors by  Theorem 1 .

Proposition 3 in  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017)  states that for the conjunctive competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            C 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , if  
                         C 
                       is floor-inclusive and closed under union, then  
                         
                            
                               C 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                            
                               C 
                            
                         
                       is also closed under union. However, this conclusion may not hold, as  Example 2  in their paper is a counterexample. By making some adjustments to  Proposition 3  in  Stefanutti and de Chiusole (2017) , we obtain the following proposition.

Proposition 8 
                         
                            If  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            is closed under union and intersection, then  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            is floor-inclusive and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                             
                            is a floor space.

If  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            is closed under union and intersection, then  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            is floor-inclusive and

                            is a floor space.

                                     ∈ 
                                     μ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              q 
                                           
                                           
                                              ′ 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     D 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     Q 
                                     ∖ 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        q 
                                        } 
                                     
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            .

According to  Anselmi et al. (2022) , any two items  
                         
                            q 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                         
                       are said to be  
                         redundant 
                       if they are associated with exactly the same competencies (i.e., if  
                         
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               q 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            μ 
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      ). Therefore, redundant items must be unnecessary items, and evidently the converse does not hold. Lemma 2 of  Anselmi et al. (2022)  shows that any irreducible competence model  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            S 
                            , 
                            C 
                            , 
                            μ 
                            ) 
                         
                       does not contain redundant items, and further illustrates that the absence of redundant items in a competence model does not imply the model is irreducible. However, in the case of conjunctiveness, the following conclusion establishes that the absence of unnecessary items is equivalent to irreducibility, and this is a direct result of  Theorem 6 .

Corollary 1 
                         
                            Under the condition of  
                            
                               Theorem  5 
                            ,  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is irreducible if and only if it does not contain unnecessary items.

Under the condition of  
                            
                               Theorem  5 
                            ,  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is irreducible if and only if it does not contain unnecessary items.

Corollary 1 establishes the existence of an irreducible competence model, the following proposition further demonstrates the uniqueness of its domain restriction.

Proposition 9 
                         
                            Under the condition of  
                            
                               Theorem  5 
                            , then there exists a unique domain restriction  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        Q 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            of  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  μ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            such that  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        Q 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ∗ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is irreducible.

Under the condition of  
                            
                               Theorem  5 
                            , then there exists a unique domain restriction

In  Example 5 , the competence models  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                       are, in fact, domain restrictions of  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      . Consequently,  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       is weakly more informative than  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                      . This leads us to systematically examine all domain restrictions of  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       to identify the unique one that is irreducible. For notational convenience in the subsequent discussion, we have introduced new labels. Please note that the models  
                         M 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               4 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       in  Example 10  correspond to  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                       in  Example 5 , respectively.

Example 10 
                         Consider a conjunctive competence model  
                               
                                  M 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     Q 
                                     , 
                                     S 
                                     , 
                                     C 
                                     , 
                                     μ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             with  
                               
                                  Q 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     1 
                                     , 
                                     2 
                                     , 
                                     3 
                                     , 
                                     4 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  S 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  C 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     0̸ 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     a 
                                     b 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            , and the mapping  
                               μ 
                             defined by  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        μ

In this paper, we primarily endeavor to elucidate the informativeness of conjunctive competence models through two distinct methodologies: the first leverages the set inclusion of floors ( Theorem 1 ), and the second involves the construction of an order embedding between two ordered sets ( Theorem 2 ). Our analysis is conducted within a subset  
                         C 
                       of the full power set  
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               S 
                            
                         
                      , subject to the constraint that  
                         C 
                       adheres to ESCC. This condition necessitates that all competencies under the skill function  
                         μ 
                       are encompassed within  
                         C 
                       (i.e.,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            C 
                         
                      ), and that  
                         C 
                       is closed under union. ESCC ensures that any floor can be represented as the union of certain competencies under  
                         μ 
                       ( Proposition 2 ), a pivotal aspect in the derivation of our principal findings. Furthermore,  Heller et al. (2017)  introduced a condition pertaining to atoms. A subset  
                         
                            A 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                         
                       is an atom at  
                         
                            s 
                            ∈ 
                            S 
                         
                       if it is a minimal set in  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               μ 
                            
                         
                       containing  
                         s 
                      . Let  
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  
                                     μ 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                       denote the set of atoms of  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               μ 
                            
                         
                      . When  
                         C 
                       satisfies the union-closure property, the conditions  
                         
                            
                               
                                  A 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        μ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            C 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            C 
                         
                       are equivalent. Consequently, we adopt a more direct approach by stipulating that  
                         
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  μ 
                               
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            C 
                         
                      .

Proposition 5 in  Heller et al. (2017)  establishes the identifiability condition (IC), which serves as both a necessary and sufficient condition for the corresponding problem function to be injective. The identifiability condition states that for all  
                         
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            C 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     T 
                                     ∈ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     T 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     } 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  ⋃ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     T 
                                     ∈ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           μ 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     T 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      implies  
                         
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         
                      . It can be observed that  
                         C 
                       and  
                         
                            ⋃

                       being injective.  Proposition 5  in  Heller et al. (2017)  is derived under a fully general setting.  Proposition 3  in this article provides a direct analogue within the ESCC framework: it employs a condition that is structurally similar to (IC) but weaker, leading correspondingly to a weaker conclusion about the problem function.

                      . This paper primarily examines dichotomous items, where responses are classified strictly as correct or incorrect. However, when extending our analysis to polytomous knowledge structures, which generalize knowledge structure theory (KST) to accommodate more than two response categories ( Heller, 2021; Stefanutti et al., 2020, 2023 ), we can further refine the definition of the mapping  
                         f 
                      , exploring these extensions and their implications will be a valuable direction for future research.

The present study primarily focuses on the conjunctive aspect of competence models. Due to the inherent duality, results for disjunctive competence models can be derived directly. However, for general competence models, the analogous conclusions fail to hold even within  
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               S 
                            
                         
                      , necessitating further research to extend the findings of this article beyond conjunctive and disjunctive cases.
